author: Dr. Lizard
title: How does nitric oxide depletion cause thrombosis in paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria?
answer: Intravascular hemolysis releases free hemoglobin, which scavenges nitric oxide, causing vasoconstriction and platelet activation.
detail: Free hemoglobin from lysed red cells binds and depletes bioavailable nitric oxide in plasma.
Nitric oxide depletion leads to smooth muscle contraction, causing abdominal pain, dysphagia, and erectile dysfunction.
Loss of nitric oxide removes its antiplatelet and vasodilatory effects, promoting thrombosis at unusual sites.
C5a from complement activation further enhances coagulation and inhibits fibrinolysis, compounding thrombotic risk.
releaseNote: Nitric oxide depletion in paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria leads to thrombosis through vasoconstriction, platelet activation, and loss of antiplatelet and vasodilatory effects.
